definition: to write down information, especially something said aloud
example: Let me take down your phone number.
The officer took down every detail of the witness's statement.
note: looked up while doing listening practice; in listening it usually means 'to record/note', not 'to dismantle'

definition: material made of a net of wire or thread with small open spaces between
example: The window has a fine mesh to keep insects out.
The bag is made of a light nylon mesh.
note: looked up during listening practice; can also be a verb meaning 'to fit/work together'

definition: to surround or cover someone or something completely
example: Flames quickly engulfed the building.
She was engulfed by a wave of sadness.
note: looked up during listening practice; often used for fire, water, or strong emotions

definition: very quickly; in a very short while
example: Don't worry, we'll be there in no time.
With the new tool, the job was done in no time.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: a large group of soldiers; also an organized group of people working together
example: The fire brigade arrived within minutes.
An army brigade was sent to the border.
note: looked up during listening practice; common in 'fire brigade'

definition: to help someone do something
example: A nurse assisted the doctor during surgery.
Can you assist me with this report?
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: a small, hard, shiny white ball formed inside an oyster, used as a jewel
example: She wore a necklace of white pearls.
Pearls form when sand gets inside an oyster's shell.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: the front of a building; also a false outward appearance hiding the truth
example: The hotel has a beautiful stone facade.
Behind his calm facade, he was very nervous.
note: looked up during listening practice; two senses — building front, and a fake outward show

definition: filled with air; or (of prices/figures) made higher than is reasonable; exaggerated
example: The prices here are really inflated.
He gave an inflated account of his own role.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: a person or company hired to do work, especially building work
example: We hired a contractor to build the garage.
The contractor finished the project ahead of schedule.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: the outside of something, especially a building
example: They painted the exterior of the house white.
The car's exterior was scratched.
note: looked up during listening practice; opposite of interior; can also be an adjective ('exterior wall')

definition: not able to be touched or measured; hard to define
example: Trust is intangible but very valuable.
The brand's reputation is an intangible asset.
note: looked up during listening practice; opposite of tangible

definition: to build or put up a structure
example: Workers are erecting a new bridge.
They erected a tent in the field.
note: looked up as 'erecting' during listening practice

definition: money paid to a worker, usually by the hour or week
example: The average wage in this city is quite high.
Workers demanded higher wages.
note: looked up as 'average wage' during listening practice

definition: easily set on fire
example: Keep flammable liquids away from the stove.
The label warns that the gas is highly flammable.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: to bravely deal with a difficult situation instead of avoiding it
example: He decided to stay and face it out.
Rather than run away, she faced it out.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: to stop using or producing something gradually, in stages
example: The company will phase out the old model next year.
They are phasing out plastic bags.
note: looked up during listening practice; opposite is 'phase in'

definition: having a special quality that makes something easy to recognize
example: The bird has a distinctive red head.
Her voice is very distinctive.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: to suddenly stop something; or to separate a piece by breaking
example: She broke off in the middle of her sentence.
He broke off a piece of chocolate.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: well known for something bad
example: The area is infamous for crime.
He is an infamous criminal.
note: looked up during listening practice; negative version of 'famous'

definition: reasonable and showing good judgment
example: That's a sensible approach to the problem.
She made a sensible decision.
note: looked up as 'sensible approach' during listening practice; note: 'sensible' = reasonable, not the same as 'sensitive'

definition: willing to obey or agree; or meeting official rules and standards
example: The new car is compliant with safety rules.
He was quiet and compliant, never arguing.
note: looked up during listening practice; common as 'compliant with' rules/standards

definition: already started and in progress
example: The project is now underway.
Preparations for the event are well underway.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: supervision or control; or a mistake made by not noticing something
example: Missing the deadline was an oversight on my part.
The committee provides oversight of the budget.
note: looked up during listening practice; two senses — supervision, and an accidental error

definition: repeatedly; many times over
example: I've told him time and time again to be careful.
Time and time again, she proved them wrong.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: giving a lot of time and effort to something; or set aside for one special purpose
example: She's a dedicated teacher who stays late every day.
This is a dedicated server for backups.
note: looked up during listening practice; two senses — hardworking/devoted, and reserved for one use

definition: a fault or weakness; a failure to reach a good standard
example: Despite its shortcomings, the plan worked.
He admitted his own shortcomings.
note: looked up during listening practice; often used in plural

definition: with regard to a particular aspect or subject
example: In terms of price, this one is better.
The plan is risky in terms of cost.
note: looked up during listening practice; used to specify which angle you're talking about

definition: to leave quickly; or to release a product hurriedly
example: She rushed out of the room in tears.
They rushed out the new phone before testing it properly.
note: looked up during listening practice; two senses — leave fast, and launch in a hurry

definition: to visit each house in turn, e.g. to sell or ask for something
example: Volunteers went door to door asking for donations.
Salesmen used to go door to door.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: a group of people chosen to make decisions or manage something
example: The committee meets every Monday.
She was elected to the planning committee.
note: looked up during listening practice; note the spelling (double m, double t, double e)

definition: an underlying meaning that is not stated directly
example: The subtext of her message was that she was angry.
There's a clear subtext of jealousy in the play.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: the act of turning; or a regular taking of turns among people or things
example: The Earth's rotation takes 24 hours.
Nurses work in rotation, changing shifts each week.
note: looked up during listening practice; two senses — spinning, and taking turns

definition: to move away; or to stop pressuring or interfering
example: Back off and give him some space.
The company backed off from the deal.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: to bring back to life, consciousness, or activity
example: Doctors managed to revive the patient.
They hope to revive the local economy.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: to extend a hand; or to contact someone to offer or ask for help
example: Feel free to reach out if you have questions.
She reached out to old friends online.
note: looked up during listening practice; very common in work emails

definition: to hold firmly in place; or to define or establish something exactly
example: It's hard to pin down the exact cause.
The wrestler pinned his opponent down.
note: looked up during listening practice; two senses — hold down, and make precise

definition: data shown by a device; or a summary report after a meeting or event
example: The screen gave a clear temperature readout.
She sent a readout of the meeting to the team.
note: looked up during listening practice; two senses — instrument display, and a post-meeting summary

definition: to fix in place with nails; or to settle or define something firmly
example: Let's nail down the date for the meeting.
We need to nail down the final price.
note: looked up during listening practice; close to 'pin down' for the 'make definite' sense

definition: a further action or contact made after something earlier
example: The doctor scheduled a follow-up next week.
I sent a follow-up email to remind them.
note: looked up during listening practice; can also be a verb 'follow up' (no hyphen)

definition: to take things out of a bag or box; or to analyze a complex idea in detail
example: Let me unpack this idea a bit more.
She unpacked her suitcase as soon as she arrived.
note: looked up as 'unpacking' during listening practice; figurative sense = explain in detail

definition: to publicly support or approve of someone or something
example: The mayor endorsed the new plan.
A famous athlete endorses the brand.
note: looked up during listening practice; used for public approval / celebrity endorsement

definition: quickly without delay; or exactly on time
example: She replied promptly to my email.
The meeting started promptly at nine.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: helping to make something happen; favorable to
example: A quiet room is conducive to studying.
The warm weather is conducive to plant growth.
note: looked up during listening practice; almost always 'conducive to'

definition: to be mentioned or arise; to approach; or (of an event) to be about to happen
example: Your name came up in the meeting.
A problem has come up.
note: looked up during listening practice; very common, several senses

definition: to criticize someone formally and severely for a fault
example: The manager reprimanded him for being late.
She was reprimanded for breaking the rules.
note: looked up as 'reprimanded' during listening practice; more formal than 'scold'

definition: to become cooler; or to calm down after being angry or excited
example: Take a walk to cool off before you reply.
Let the soup cool off a little.
note: looked up during listening practice; can describe temperature, emotions, or fading enthusiasm

definition: to move back to a previous position or state; to switch back
example: Let's shift back to the original plan.
The seat shifted back when he leaned on it.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: to take goods to someone; or to give a speech; or to do what you promised
example: They deliver pizza to your door.
The CEO delivered an inspiring speech and delivered on her promises.
note: looked up during listening practice; senses — take to, give (speech), and fulfill (a promise)

definition: deep sadness, especially after someone dies
example: She was overcome with grief at the funeral.
It took him years to get over his grief.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: strong anger and shock; or an act that causes this
example: The decision caused public outrage.
People expressed outrage at the cruel attack.
note: looked up during listening practice; can also be a verb ('the news outraged everyone')

definition: to say words aloud for someone to write down; or to command or control something
example: She dictated the letter to her assistant.
The weather will dictate our plans for the day.
note: looked up as 'dictating' during listening practice; two senses — say for writing, and control/decide

definition: to remain outside or away; to not get involved
example: Stay out of it — it's not your problem.
The kids stayed out until dark.
note: looked up during listening practice; 'stay out of it' = don't interfere

definition: a hand with the fingers closed tightly together
example: He banged his fist on the table.
She raised her fist in protest.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: used to say what is actually true, in contrast to how things seem
example: He seems confident, but in reality he's shy.
In reality, the project took twice as long.
note: looked up during listening practice; corrects or contrasts with appearances

definition: the power believed to control events; or what finally happens to someone
example: No one knows what fate has in store.
The court will decide his fate tomorrow.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: a short speech meant to encourage someone or boost confidence
example: The coach gave the team a pep talk before the game.
I needed a quick pep talk before the interview.
note: looked up during listening practice; informal

definition: a structure with a square base and triangular sides meeting at a point; or a structure shaped like this
example: We visited the pyramids in Egypt.
The company has a clear pyramid structure.
note: looked up during listening practice; also used figuratively for hierarchies

definition: a thought or interest that fills your mind and you cannot stop thinking about
example: Her obsession with cleanliness drives everyone crazy.
Football is his only obsession.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: to formally admit in court that you committed the crime
example: He pleaded guilty to theft.
She decided to plead guilty and avoid a trial.
note: looked up during listening practice; legal term; opposite is 'plead not guilty'

definition: to break with a sharp sound or form a line of damage; or to solve/break a difficult problem or code
example: The glass cracked when I dropped it.
They finally cracked the code.
note: looked up during listening practice; also a noun ('a crack in the wall'); 'crack a problem' = solve it

definition: to take back something you said; or to pull something back in
example: He was forced to retract his statement.
The cat retracted its claws.
note: looked up during listening practice; senses — take back words, and pull in

definition: a painful feeling of guilt or embarrassment; or a pity (a regrettable thing)
example: What a shame you can't come!
She felt deep shame about the lie.
note: looked up during listening practice; 'What a shame!' = how unfortunate

definition: deep regret for something wrong you have done
example: He showed no remorse for his crime.
She was filled with remorse afterwards.
note: looked up during listening practice; stronger than ordinary regret

definition: to avoid a problem or question; or to step to one side to avoid something
example: The politician sidestepped the question.
He sidestepped to dodge the ball.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: to stop or come to an end (formal)
example: The rain finally ceased.
The two sides agreed to cease fire.
note: looked up during listening practice; formal; common in 'ceasefire'

definition: to give in or withdraw a demand or position in an argument
example: He refused to back down.
The company backed down after public pressure.
note: looked up during listening practice; admitting defeat in a dispute

definition: something said; a comment
example: She made a rude remark.
His remarks upset the audience.
note: looked up during listening practice; also a verb ('he remarked that...')

definition: done to get revenge or hit back at someone
example: The country imposed retaliatory tariffs.
It was a retaliatory attack.
note: looked up during listening practice; common in 'retaliatory tariffs/measures'

definition: to remove a covering or layer; to take away; (noun) a long narrow piece
example: They stripped the paint off the door.
He was stripped of his medals.
note: looked up during listening practice; also a noun ('a strip of paper')

definition: equal in value, amount, or meaning
example: A mile is roughly equivalent to 1.6 kilometers.
There's no English equivalent for this word.
note: looked up during listening practice; also a noun ('the equivalent of')

definition: an amount left over after needs are met; more than is needed
example: The country has a trade surplus.
We have a surplus of food this year.
note: looked up during listening practice; opposite of deficit; also an adjective ('surplus stock')

definition: the amount by which spending is greater than income; a shortage
example: The government is trying to cut the budget deficit.
They have a trade deficit with China.
note: looked up during listening practice; opposite of surplus

definition: naturally belonging to a place; native (of people, plants, or animals)
example: Kangaroos are indigenous to Australia.
The museum honors indigenous peoples.
note: looked up during listening practice; 'indigenous people' = original inhabitants

definition: a public procession of people or vehicles; a military review
example: We watched the New Year parade.
Soldiers marched in the military parade.
note: looked up during listening practice; 'military parade' = 阅兵

definition: a person who loves and strongly supports their country
example: He was a true patriot.
The patriots fought to defend their homeland.
note: looked up the English for 爱国者; adjective is 'patriotic'

definition: the process of finding and hiring new people for a job or group
example: The company has paused recruitment.
Army recruitment has fallen this year.
note: looked up during listening practice; verb is 'recruit'

definition: the state of being liked or supported by many people
example: The show's popularity grew quickly.
His popularity among voters is rising.
note: looked up during listening practice

definition: to work together secretly to do something wrong or deceive others
example: The two companies colluded to fix prices.
They were accused of colluding with the enemy.
note: explained while translating a sentence; noun is 'collusion'

definition: a spoken or written description and analysis of an event as it happens
example: He gave live commentary on the match.
The book offers a commentary on modern life.
note: looked up during listening practice; common for sports/news 'live commentary'

definition: annoyed or discouraged because you cannot do or get what you want
example: I'm frustrated with the slow internet.
She felt frustrated by the constant delays.
note: looked up during listening practice; verb 'frustrate', noun 'frustration'

definition: to arrive or appear, especially at an arranged place or event
example: He didn't show up for the meeting.
Only half the guests showed up.
note: looked up during listening practice; informal

definition: the act of breaking a rule or law; or an act that disrespects something
example: He got a parking violation.
It was a clear violation of human rights.
note: looked up during listening practice; verb is 'violate'

definition: a committee with official duties; or a fee paid for selling; or the act of committing (a crime)
example: She earns a 10% commission on each sale.
A commission was set up to investigate the accident.
note: looked up during listening practice; several senses — body, sales fee, and 'commission a work'

definition: to protect from attack; or to argue in support of someone or something
example: Soldiers defended the city.
She defended her decision in the meeting.
note: looked up during listening practice; senses — protect, and argue for; noun 'defense'

definition: the quality of being exact and accurate
example: The parts are made with great precision.
She answered with precision.
note: looked up during listening practice; adjective is 'precise'

definition: a penalty for breaking a rule (often plural); or official permission or approval
example: The US imposed sanctions on the country.
The plan needs the board's sanction.
note: looked up during listening practice; note the two opposite senses — punish vs. approve

definition: a place where roads meet; (often 'crossroads') a point where an important choice must be made
example: Turn left at the crossroad.
Her career is at a crossroads.
note: looked up during listening practice; figurative 'at a crossroads' = decisive moment

definition: the quality of being trusted and believed in
example: The scandal damaged his credibility.
The report lacks credibility.
note: looked up during listening practice; adjective is 'credible'

definition: a vote by the public to decide whether to remove an elected official before their term ends
example: Voters demanded a recall vote against the mayor.
The governor survived the recall vote.
note: looked up during listening practice; here 'recall' = remove from office

definition: the act of thinking about a decision or matter again
example: The plan is under reconsideration.
She asked the court for reconsideration of the ruling.
note: looked up during listening practice; verb is 'reconsider'

definition: making you think something is wrong; or feeling that someone is guilty or untrustworthy
example: The police found a suspicious package.
She was suspicious of his motives.
note: looked up during listening practice; two senses — looks dodgy, and feels distrustful

definition: relating to surgery; or done with great precision
example: She needs surgical treatment.
The army carried out a surgical strike on the base.
note: looked up during listening practice; 'surgical strike' = a precise, targeted attack

definition: working secretly, hiding your true identity to get information
example: He is an undercover police officer.
She went undercover to investigate the gang.
note: looked up during listening practice; can also be an adverb ('go undercover')

definition: to change or correct something; or (British) to study again for an exam
example: We had to revise our plans.
She's revising for her history exam.
note: looked up during listening practice; the 'study for exam' sense is British English

definition: located in the sea away from the coast; or based in a foreign country (often for tax)
example: They are building an offshore wind farm.
He hid money in an offshore account.
note: looked up during listening practice; two senses — out at sea, and based abroad

definition: a spy who works secretly inside an organization; also a small dark spot on the skin, or a small burrowing animal
example: There was a mole leaking secrets to the press.
She has a small mole on her cheek.
note: looked up during listening practice; the 'inside spy' sense is the non-obvious one

definition: a formal court decision that someone is guilty; or a firmly held belief
example: He has a previous conviction for theft.
She spoke with great conviction.
note: looked up during listening practice; two senses — guilty verdict, and strong belief; verb 'convict'

definition: the person who plans and directs a clever or complex scheme; (verb) to plan such a scheme
example: He was the mastermind behind the robbery.
She masterminded the whole campaign.
note: looked up during listening practice; often used for the brain behind a crime or plan

definition: secret cooperation to do something wrong or to deceive others
example: The firms acted in collusion to raise prices.
There was collusion between the officials.
note: looked up during listening practice; noun form of 'collude'

definition: the official handing over of an accused person to another country to be tried
example: The US requested his extradition.
There is no extradition treaty between the two countries.
note: looked up during listening practice; verb is 'extradite'

definition: supporting democracy and democratic reform
example: Thousands joined the pro-democracy protests.
She is a well-known pro-democracy activist.
note: looked up during listening practice; prefix 'pro-' = in favor of

definition: to go around or avoid something; (noun) a road around a town, or a heart operation
example: You can bypass the city center by taking this road.
The new system bypasses the usual approval process.
note: looked up during listening practice; also a noun (road / heart bypass surgery)

definition: (of an act) unfair, unjust, or against the law
example: He sued the company for wrongful dismissal.
She was freed after a wrongful conviction.
note: looked up while translating; common in 'wrongful dismissal/conviction/death'

definition: a statement admitting you have done something wrong or committed a crime
example: The suspect signed a confession.
His confession led to a quick conviction.
note: looked up while translating; verb is 'confess'

definition: to cause something to start; to trigger a reaction or feeling
example: The new tax sparked widespread protests.
A single comment sparked the whole argument.
note: looked up as 'sparked' while translating; also a noun ('a spark of interest')

definition: a person's family name, shared with relatives
example: Please write your surname first.
'Smith' is a very common English surname.
note: looked up while translating; opposite of 'first name / given name'

definition: to formally accuse someone of a crime
example: He was charged with theft.
Police charged her with dangerous driving.
note: looked up while translating; legal term; followed by the offence ('charge sb with murder')

definition: done on purpose, not by accident
example: The foul was clearly intentional.
Her rudeness wasn't intentional.
note: looked up while translating; opposite of 'accidental'; adverb 'intentionally'

definition: the crime of killing a person
example: He was charged with homicide.
Detectives are treating the death as a homicide.
note: looked up while translating; legal/police term covering intentional or accidental killing

definition: a lasting feeling of resentment over a past insult or wrong
example: She holds a grudge against her old boss.
He's not the type to bear a grudge.
note: looked up while translating; common in 'hold/bear a grudge'

definition: a second trial of a case that has already been judged once
example: The judge ordered a retrial.
New evidence led to a retrial.
note: looked up while translating; prefix 're-' = again

definition: to look at or consider something again carefully
example: We need to re-examine the evidence.
The lawyer asked to re-examine the witness.
note: looked up as 're-examining' while translating

definition: to move or climb quickly with difficulty; or to mix something up into disorder
example: They scrambled up the rocky hill.
The message was scrambled so no one could read it.
note: looked up as 'scrambled' while translating; senses — struggle/rush, and jumble/encrypt

definition: dishonest or of doubtful honesty; (literally) giving or full of shade
example: I don't trust him — he seems shady.
He made some shady business deals.
note: looked up while translating; the 'dishonest' sense is the non-obvious one

definition: a dishonest scheme to cheat people out of money
example: He lost money in an online scam.
Be careful of phone scams.
note: looked up while translating; also a verb ('they scammed him out of $500')

definition: systems for keeping places clean and for disposing of sewage and waste
example: Poor sanitation spreads disease.
The city improved its water and sanitation.
note: looked up while translating; 'sanitation worker' = 环卫工人

definition: to admit someone to hospital for treatment
example: He was hospitalized after the accident.
She had to be hospitalized for a week.
note: looked up as 'hospitalized' while translating; usually passive ('be hospitalized')

definition: to continue or go ahead with something; to move forward
example: Let's proceed with the plan.
Please proceed to gate 12.
note: looked up while translating; slightly formal; note 'proceeds' (noun) = money from a sale
